competition.narrative: Arnott's is currently using three incumbent systems: Smart Hub for S2P (primary competitor and displacement target), SAP BPX for catalog management, and Symphony for another procurement module, per the 9 April 2026 demo call with Ali Khan. Ali stated he 'prefers one unified system', which means Zycus is competing against the incumbents' collective installed base and the buyer's status-quo inertia, plus potentially against each incumbent's proposal to expand and unify their own footprint (e.g., Smart Hub offering to add modules). Existing Smart Hub contracts run through 2027/2028, which could create contract-termination cost or approval friction, though Ali's unification preference signals it is not a blocker. No other external competitors (e.g., Coupa, Jaggaer, GEP) have been named yet, but the TAG-led market scan and imminent RFI suggest a multi-vendor evaluation is underway. The wedge is unification: Zycus offers a single, native S2C platform (iContract, iSource, iSave, iSupplier, iRisk) vs the buyer's current patchwork, which creates inefficiency, higher TCO, and integration overhead. The how-we-win is to position Zycus as the only true unified alternative in the RFI response, present a clear migration and ROI plan that neutralizes the Smart Hub contract concern, and demonstrate APAC regional support strength (Ali's explicit ask) to differentiate from incumbents that may have weaker Asia coverage. If other best-of-breed competitors enter (Coupa, GEP), Zycus must emphasize depth in S2C vs their breadth, and the Merlin AI story if the buyer has AI appetite (no AIS score available to confirm).

He stated he 'prefers one unified system', signaling the core pain is operational complexity, inefficiency, and likely higher total cost of ownership from managing multiple disparate tools. The pain is owned by the procurement organization (Ali Khan is the Category Lead, Tom Vicars is the CPO, Ishana Govender is the COE Director), and the 2027 unification goal suggests the pain is acknowledged and prioritized but not acute or crisis-driven. No quantified impact has been shared (no cost, headcount, cycle-time, or error-rate data), so the pain remains qualitative. The existing Smart Hub contracts through 2027/2028 indicate the buyer is not in a forced-change scenario (no contract expiration driving urgency), which lowers the compelling-event pressure. The pain is real and confirmed, but without quantification or a near-term forcing function, it is a 'nice to solve' rather than 'must solve now' pain, which aligns with the moderate urgency and 2027 timeline. Quantifying the cost of fragmentation and tying it to a business outcome (e.g., savings target, faster approvals, compliance risk reduction) in the RFI response will elevate the pain and create a stronger business case.

Proactively presenting a phased migration plan and ROI case (cost/risk of fragmented status quo vs Smart Hub sunk cost) in the RFI response or early post-RFI call will neutralize this before it becomes a blocker.

north_star_verdict.math: 151 days to close (27 November 2026). Deal created 10 April 2026, 80 days ago. In Qualified stage for approximately 80 days with no submission-date progression. RFI expected 15 June 2026 per Next Step, now 11 days overdue with no update. A typical enterprise S2P motion from RFI through close requires 24-33 weeks (168-231 days): RFI response 2-3 weeks, RFP round 3-4 weeks, shortlisting 2 weeks, ShoeFit/BRD 2 weeks, demos/workshops 3-4 weeks, commercials 2-3 weeks, EB/CFO proposal 2 weeks, InfoSec/integration review 3-4 weeks, contracting/MSA/SOW 4-6 weeks. Even if RFI issued today (29 June), earliest realistic close is late December 2026 to February 2027. Current 27 November close date requires the buyer to compress the cycle by approximately 30-40 percent, which is unlikely in a committee-driven procurement with four mapped stakeholders and incumbent displacement. Buyer's stated 2027 unification goal and existing Smart Hub contracts through 2027/2028 suggest the deal is naturally a 2027 event. Forward slip of 60-90 days is probable unless the buyer accelerates post-RFI. No forward slips recorded yet (deal still in original Qualified stage), but the math does not support the current forecast.

flags: Convert Ali Khan from evaluator to active coach — equip him with quantified ROI (cost of manual processing today, savings from unified S2P, peer CPG references, risk mitigation proof points) he can champion internally to Ishana Govender and Tom Vicars (CPO, EB) before the RFI shortlist is set. Ali asked detailed capability questions on the 9 April call (risk assessment, contract clause analysis, PDF classification) showing he is building the internal business case, but he has not yet demonstrated advocacy. The next step is to give him the ammunition to sell Zycus internally: a tailored ROI model (e.g., '25% reduction in manual procurement processing time, $X contract compliance risk avoided, supplier risk visibility across 500+ vendors'), 2-3 named CPG customer references (ideally APAC, similar scale/complexity), and a Merlin/I2O AI-powered procurement narrative if AI appetite exists (no AIS data, but the buyer's focus on automation and data analytics per Azura's 9 April comments suggests openness). Schedule a deep-dive with Ali by early July 2026 to deliver this and test his willingness to introduce us to Ishana/Tom.

Gain early competitive intelligence — the RFI (expected 15 June, now 11 days overdue) will surface the shortlist, but waiting passively until RFI issuance risks being blindsided by competitor positioning. Ask TAG (the partner channel) and Ali Khan directly in the next touchpoint (within 7 days): 'Who else are you evaluating for the unified S2P platform?' and 'What criteria will differentiate the shortlist and scoring model?' Then position Zycus' wedge BEFORE the RFI is formally issued: the integrated full-suite S2P platform (iContract + iSource + iRisk + iSupplier + iSave) with AI-powered risk clause analysis and holistic supplier risk management (which Ali specifically asked about on 9 April) vs likely modular or best-of-breed competitors (SAP Ariba, Coupa, GEP, Ivalua, Jaggaer are typical but unconfirmed). Emphasize the single-platform advantage that directly addresses the buyer's pain (fragmented systems, manual workflows) and 2027 unification goal, and contrast with competitors' multi-module/integration complexity.

Proactively neutralize the Smart Hub contract inertia — Ali stated on the 9 April call that he prefers one unified system despite existing Smart Hub contracts through 2027/2028, signaling the contract tenure is a known hurdle but not a blocker. The buyer's 2027 unification goal and stated pain with fragmented systems create urgency, but the transition mechanics (early termination cost, parallel run, migration timeline, data migration plan) have not been addressed and could become a late-stage objection or delay the close into 2027. The next step is to present a phased migration plan and ROI case in the RFI response or early post-RFI call: show that the cost/risk of continuing the fragmented status quo (manual processing cost, data inaccuracy, lack of contract authoring, supplier risk blind spots) outweighs the Smart Hub contract sunk cost, and propose a transition approach (e.g., parallel run iContract/iSource/iRisk in H2 2026 while Smart Hub winds down through 2027, or negotiate early Smart Hub termination offset by Zycus faster time-to-value). Tie the migration timeline to the buyer's 2027 unification deadline to make it a forcing function, not an excuse to delay.
